narrative:
As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and personal factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 39, first-year school decile of 1, third-year school decile of 1, and plan to attend full-time—and estimated a 62.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates with stronger signals of bar success.

The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your third-year school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's average of around 5.5 and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Coming in second was your first-year school decile of 1, also far below the average of about 5.7, which reinforced concerns about consistent academic standing from early on. While these low decile rankings strongly pulled the prediction toward failure, your LSAT score of 39—higher than the average of 36.7—had the next biggest impact by pushing in the opposite direction, suggesting potential for success that partially offset the other weaknesses.

Compared to typical students in the dataset, where full-time enrollment is the norm at over 92%, your status there didn't stand out, nor did your family income bracket sway things much. Overall, your profile showed strengths like that solid LSAT but was overshadowed by early and later undergraduate performance that placed you in the bottom decile, making you riskier than most applicants who pass the bar on their first try. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle-class background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements.

To move forward, focus on bolstering your academic record, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate program or additional coursework to demonstrate upward trends beyond those low deciles. Retaking the LSAT to push even higher could amplify its positive influence, and gathering strong letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience would help contextualize your story. Consider reapplying next cycle after addressing these gaps, or exploring bridge programs designed for students with non-traditional paths to build bar-exam readiness. With deliberate steps, you can shift the odds in your favor for future opportunities.
